food_subgroup: Layer cake — pandan sponge cake with coconut gula melaka cream and toasted desiccated coconut coating

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Ondeh Ondeh Cake. A0GBQ (layer cake with coconut cream) is the closest defensible parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of Ondeh Ondeh Cake as sold by Bengawan Solo weighs approximately 100g and provides approximately 360 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Ondeh Ondeh Cake — HPB Singapore Foodo database + Bengawan Solo product label
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) + Bengawan Solo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database for pandan layer cake and corroborated against Bengawan Solo product label data. No exact HPB entry for Ondeh Ondeh Cake exists; values represent the closest HPB-anchored adaptation for a pandan sponge layer cake with coconut cream frosting and toasted desiccated coconut. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) used as secondary cross-check for individual component macros (cake flour, coconut cream, butter, desiccated coconut).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sponge Cake and Coconut Cream Analogues — University of Sydney GI Database (closest analogous estimate: vanilla sponge cake GI ~46–67; refined wheat flour cake GI ~65)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ondeh Ondeh Cake. GI ~65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for conventional sponge cake made with refined wheat flour (GI range 46–67, midpoint ~65). The pandan sponge layers use cake flour (refined wheat), eggs, sugar, and butter — compositionally equivalent to a standard sponge cake. The coconut gula melaka cream frosting adds fat which moderates glycaemic response marginally, but the dominant GI driver is the refined wheat flour sponge. Gula melaka (palm sugar) has a GI of approximately 40–55, slightly lower than sucrose (~65), but the quantity used does not materially lower the overall GI of the composite cake. GL per 100g serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 44) / 100 = 28.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat (10g per 100g slice = ~50% of daily saturated fat limit); sodium is moderate at 120mg per slice
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content (120mg per slice) is moderate and not the primary cardiovascular concern; however, the high saturated fat load (10g per slice, predominantly lauric and myristic acid from coconut cream plus palmitic acid from butter) contributes to LDL-C elevation and endothelial dysfunction — hypertensive patients should limit to occasional small portions and prioritise overall saturated fat budget.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (32g per 100g slice) and medium-high GI (~65) with GL of 28.6 per slice
type2_diabetes.guidance: A single 100g slice delivers 32g of sugar (gula melaka + caster sugar) and a glycaemic load of 28.6 — well above the recommended per-meal GL ceiling of 10–20 for T2DM management; the fat content (18g) provides modest glycaemic moderation but does not offset the sugar load, and this cake should be avoided or strictly limited to a maximum of one small slice (~50g) on rare festive occasions with corresponding carbohydrate reduction elsewhere in the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 10g per slice (coconut cream lauric/myristic acid + butter palmitic acid) and high sugar 32g per slice driving triglyceride elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of 10g saturated fat (predominantly from coconut cream and butter) and 32g free sugars per slice poses a dual cardiovascular risk — saturated fatty acids (lauric C12:0, myristic C14:0, palmitic C16:0) raise LDL-C and activate pro-atherogenic pathways, while the high sugar load elevates postprandial triglycerides; individuals with established CVD or high cardiovascular risk should treat this as an occasional festive food only, limiting to one small slice (~50g) infrequently.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory double hit: 32g sugar (drives acute hyperglycaemia and cytokine release) + 10g saturated fat (lauric and myristic acid activate TLR4-mediated inflammatory pathways)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should severely limit consumption — the combination of 32g sugar and 10g coconut/butter saturated fat per slice represents one of the most pro-inflammatory nutritional profiles in the Singapore cake repertoire; maximum one small slice (~50g) on rare festive occasions is the clinical recommendation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ondeh Ondeh Cake is a modern Singaporean layer cake inspired by the traditional ondeh ondeh glutinous rice ball. It consists of alternating layers of pandan-flavoured sponge cake (made with refined wheat flour, eggs, butter, and caster sugar, coloured and flavoured with pandan leaf extract) sandwiched with a rich coconut gula melaka cream frosting, and coated on the exterior with toasted desiccated coconut. The cake was popularised by Bengawan Solo bakery in Singapore and has become a national food icon, particularly associated with Hari Raya and Chinese New Year celebrations. KEY DISTINCTION: Unlike the traditional ondeh ondeh ball (glutinous rice ball with liquid gula melaka filling rolled in shredded coconut), the cake version contains no glutinous rice — this gives it a GI of approximately 65 (medium-high) rather than ~80 (high) for the traditional ball. Per 100g (one standard Bengawan Solo slice): approximately 360 kcal, 5g protein, 18g fat (10g saturated fat from coconut cream and butter), 44g carbohydrate (32g sugar from gula melaka and caster sugar), 1.5g dietary fibre, 120mg sodium. Glycaemic load per slice is 28.6 — clinically significant for diabetes management. The high saturated fat (10g) and high free sugar (32g) per slice make this a high-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease, and Rheumatoid Arthritis. Taste profile: intensely fragrant pandan aroma, rich coconut-caramel sweetness from gula melaka, moist sponge texture, with a slightly chewy toasted coconut exterior. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo database adapted for Bengawan Solo product.
common_user_queries: ondeh ondeh cake vs ondeh ondeh ball
ondeh ondeh cake GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for Ondeh Ondeh Cake as a named dish. Values are adapted from HPB pandan layer cake data and corroborated against Bengawan Solo product label information and USDA FoodData Central component data for cake flour, coconut cream, butter, and desiccated coconut. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using conventional sponge cake as the closest approved comparator. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct approved-source entry for this specific product.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Ondeh Ondeh Cake (pandan sponge + coconut cream + butter composite) is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of April 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific composite cake is not reported in available approved sources. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: pandan extract provides negligible ascorbic acid; confirmed as effectively zero per HPB Singapore data for comparable pandan cakes. 4. GI ~65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (conventional sponge cake, refined wheat flour base); no direct GI measurement for Ondeh Ondeh Cake exists in the approved GI literature. GL per 100g serving = (65 × 44) / 100 = 28.6. 5. This cake is NOT the same as traditional ondeh ondeh balls (glutinous rice, GI ~80) — the absence of glutinous rice is the key compositional distinction. 6. Bengawan Solo product label corroborates the HPB-adapted values; the label is treated as a secondary supporting source, not a primary approved source under Project Veritas source policy.
